1: Samuel's farewell speech begins with a public accountability review: 'I have listened to you in all that you have said to me and have set a king over you.' Samuel acknowledges his compliance with the people's demand — he is not revisiting the question of whether the kingship was right but moving to the terms of accountability that now govern it. The speech functions as a covenant lawsuit (rîḇ) — a juridical form used when YHWH or his representative brings charges against the covenant people.
2: 'Now behold, the king walks before you, and I am old and gray, and behold, my sons are with you. I have walked before you from my youth until this day.' Samuel's intergenerational framing — the king is young and beginning, Samuel is old and ending — establishes the transition moment. His sons' presence in the assembly is a loaded reference: they were the corrupt judges of 8:1-3 whose failure precipitated the demand for a king. Samuel does not hide this history.
3: 'Here I am; testify against me before YHWH and before his anointed. Whose ox have I taken? Whose donkey have I taken? Whom have I defrauded? Whom have I oppressed? From whose hand have I taken a bribe to blind my eyes with it?' The rhetorical questions constitute a covenant integrity oath — Samuel invokes YHWH and the king as witnesses to his clean record. The specific items (ox, donkey, bribe) correspond to the abuses Samuel will soon describe as the pattern of kings (8:14-17). Samuel's governance was the alternative to what kings do.
4: The people respond: 'You have not defrauded us or oppressed us or taken anything from any man's hand.' The unanimous public acquittal is Samuel's covenant credential — he exercised authority without extracting tribute. The contrast with the corrupt sons of 8:3 and the projected abuses of royal government (8:11-18) is pointed. The people's verdict legitimizes Samuel's authority to now speak covenant law to them.
5: 'YHWH is witness against you, and his anointed is witness this day, that you have not found anything in my hand.' And they said, 'He is witness.' The double-witness formula (YHWH + the king) satisfies Deuteronomy's requirement for two or three witnesses (Deut 19:15). Samuel has established the court's legitimacy before delivering the covenant address.
6: Samuel continues: 'YHWH is witness, who appointed Moses and Aaron and who brought your fathers up from the land of Egypt.' The saving-history section (vv6-13) begins with the Exodus — the founding act of covenant relationship. Samuel's survey will move from Moses/Aaron through the judges to the present, building the case that YHWH has been Israel's constant deliverer while Israel has been a constant covenant-breaker.
7: 'Now therefore stand still so that I may plead with you before YHWH concerning all the righteous deeds (ṣidqôṯ) of YHWH that he did for you and for your fathers.' Ṣidqôṯ YHWH — YHWH's righteous acts/saving acts — is a technical term in the Psalms and prophets for YHWH's interventions on behalf of the covenant people. Samuel frames YHWH's history as a record of consistent faithfulness; he will then frame Israel's history as a record of consistent apostasy. The contrast is the foundation of the covenant lawsuit.
8: 'When Jacob went into Egypt and your fathers cried out to YHWH, then YHWH sent Moses and Aaron, who brought your fathers out of Egypt and made them dwell in this place.' The saving-history summary compresses Exodus through Settlement into a single verse — the characteristic Deuteronomistic credo form (cf. Deut 26:5-9; Josh 24:2-13). The credo functions as covenant memory: this is what YHWH did, which is why covenant obligation exists.
9: But they forgot YHWH their God, and he sold them into the hand of Sisera the commander of Jabin's army and into the hand of the Philistines and into the hand of the king of Moab. The šāḵaḥ (forget) of covenant unfaithfulness is paired with mākar (sold/delivered over) — YHWH's judicial abandonment of the people to their enemies is the consequence of forgetting him. The three oppressors listed (Sisera, Philistines, Moab) cover major episodes of the book of Judges.
10: They cried out to YHWH: 'We have sinned, because we have forsaken YHWH and served the Baals and the Ashtaroth. Now deliver us from the hand of our enemies and we will serve you.' The covenant repentance formula is identical to the cycle pattern in Judges (e.g., Judg 10:10-16): acknowledgment of sin, identification of the sin (serving Baals and Ashtaroth), petition for deliverance, pledge of renewed service. Samuel is identifying the present moment as yet another instance of the recurring cycle.
11: YHWH sent Jerubbaal (Gideon), Bedan, Jephthah, and Samuel himself to deliver Israel. The list of judges includes 'Bedan' — an otherwise unknown figure; some manuscripts read 'Barak' here (cf. LXX). Samuel's inclusion of himself in the list of deliverers is not self-aggrandizement but covenantal continuity: his office is in the same sequence as the judges, the last expression of the charismatic-deliverer pattern before the monarchy.
12: 'And when you saw that Nahash the king of the Ammonites came against you, you said to me, No, but a king shall reign over us, when YHWH your God was your king.' The juxtaposition is sharp: the Ammonite threat (just resolved in ch 11) was the stated reason for demanding a king. But YHWH was already their king, and the judges-pattern of deliverance was still operative. Israel's demand was not practical necessity but an act of covenant rejection.
13: 'And now behold the king whom you have chosen, for whom you have asked; behold, YHWH has set a king over you.' The double hinnēh (behold) and the double acknowledgment — you chose/you asked — holds the people accountable while affirming that YHWH has fulfilled the request. The king they wanted is now their king; YHWH has conceded while maintaining the record of the demand's faithlessness. The tension between divine disapproval and divine concession is unresolved — it characterizes the entire monarchic history.
14: The conditional covenant formula: 'If you will fear YHWH and serve him and obey his voice and not rebel against the commandment of YHWH, and if both you and the king who reigns over you will follow YHWH your God, it will be well.' The introduction of the king into the covenant formula is the new element: monarchy does not replace the Sinai covenant but is incorporated under it. Both people and king are now equally subject to covenant obligation. The blessing-conditional is open: the monarchy can still succeed if covenant faithfulness is maintained.
15: 'But if you will not obey the voice of YHWH and will rebel against the commandment of YHWH, then the hand of YHWH will be against you and your king.' The curse-conditional mirrors the blessing but adds 'and your king' — the monarchy participates in the covenant curse as well as the blessing. This is the theological framework for every subsequent royal evaluation in Samuel-Kings: kings are judged by covenant faithfulness, and the people's fate is tied to their king's obedience.
16: 'Now therefore stand and see this great thing that YHWH will do before your eyes.' Samuel invites the assembled Israel to witness a covenant sign. The invitation to 'stand and see' (hiṯyaṣṣəḇû ûrəʾû) is a theophanic summons — the language used when YHWH is about to act visibly. The covenant sign will be thunder and rain (v17-18) — unusual weather at wheat harvest, which occurs in the dry season (May-June) when rain is abnormal in Palestine.
17: 'Is it not wheat harvest today? I will call upon YHWH, that he may send thunder and rain. And you shall know and see that your wickedness is great, which you have done in the sight of YHWH, in asking for yourselves a king.' The thunder-at-harvest is a meteorological sign of divine displeasure: wheat harvest in ancient Canaan/Israel falls in May-June during the dry season; unseasonal rain and thunder would be unmistakable as a divine message. Samuel's ability to summon such a sign confirms his prophetic authority.
18: Samuel calls on YHWH, and YHWH sends thunder and rain that day. The entire people greatly fears YHWH and Samuel. The combined fear of YHWH and Samuel indicates that the sign achieved its purpose: the people recognize both divine reality and prophetic authority in a single event. The thunder-and-rain sign is the covenant lawsuit's climactic 'exhibit': YHWH testifies in the natural world.
19: The people say to Samuel: 'Pray for your servants to YHWH your God, that we may not die, for we have added to all our sins this evil, to ask for ourselves a king.' The combination of awe and self-condemnation is the desired covenant response — they acknowledge the sin, not merely the fear. Their appeal to Samuel's intercession ('pray for your servants') acknowledges that they need a mediator whose relationship with YHWH can withstand their guilt. The phrase 'we have added to all our sins this evil' is covenant self-indictment.
20: Samuel's consolation: 'Do not be afraid; you have done all this evil. Yet do not turn aside from following YHWH, but serve YHWH with all your heart.' The pastoral grace of this verse is extraordinary: Samuel does not minimize the sin ('you have done all this evil') but immediately pivots to the path forward. The covenant's conditional threats do not preclude continued covenant relationship; repentance and ongoing service are still available. Fear is not the goal; covenant faithfulness is.
21: 'And do not turn aside after empty things (haṯṯōhû) that cannot profit or deliver, for they are empty.' Tōhû — void, emptiness, the word used in Genesis 1:2 for formless creation — describes the idols and political alternatives that Israel might pursue. The contrast between YHWH who delivers and the tōhû-things that cannot is Samuel's final anti-idolatry warning. The word that described pre-creation emptiness describes the religious alternatives to YHWH.
22: 'For YHWH will not abandon his people, for his great name's sake, because it has pleased YHWH to make you a people for himself.' The ground of covenant security is entirely YHWH's self-commitment: not Israel's merit, not the monarchy's strength, but YHWH's own name (šəmô haggāḏôl) and his pleasure (hôʾîl YHWH) in making Israel his people. The double grounding — name and pleasure — is the irreversible foundation. YHWH's reputation is at stake in Israel's survival; he cannot abandon his people without abandoning his own identity.
23: 'Moreover, as for me, far be it from me that I should sin against YHWH by ceasing to pray for you, and I will instruct you in the good and the right way.' Samuel's pledge of continued intercession is his covenant commitment: even in retirement from the judgeship, he will not withdraw prayer. To 'cease to pray' (ḥāḏal mippallēl) is declared a sin against YHWH — prayer for the people is a covenant obligation, not an optional pastoral extra. Samuel's identity as prophet-intercessor will persist beyond political power.
24: 'Only fear YHWH and serve him faithfully with all your heart, for consider what great things he has done for you.' The closing call synthesizes the entire speech: covenant fear, covenant service, whole-hearted devotion, grounded in grateful memory of YHWH's saving acts. The rāʾû (see/consider) returns to the eyewitness theme — they have just seen YHWH act in thunder and rain; let them see the full scope of his saving history and respond accordingly.
25: 'But if you still do wickedly, you shall be swept away, both you and your king.' The speech ends with the curse-conditional in its starkest form — 'swept away' (tîssāpû) — and includes the king in the potential catastrophe. The final verse is the prophecy of the monarchy's potential failure before the monarchy has ruled a year. The book of Samuel will take the rest of its pages demonstrating that this warning was not heeded, and that even the best of Israel's kings — David — falls into the wickedness that leads toward the sweep.
